Lucas Dewey
Experience Contact
Back to experience

2022-2023

BrightDrop (General Motors)

Senior Mechanical Design Engineer

Led mechanical and electromechanical development for electric delivery cart and van platforms, spanning caster brake systems, structural validation, sealed electronics enclosures, cabling interfaces, and grocery temperature-control concepts from early R&D through prototype validation.

Location Palo Alto, CA

Highlights

  • Supported Trace Grocery cart and Zevo electric delivery vehicle platforms across cart safety, electronics packaging, and delivery-use mechanical systems.
  • Owned electromechanical prototyping, control-logic integration, FEA validation, and DFM-focused enclosure design in Siemens NX.
  • Reduced system complexity and cost by eliminating manual drawer latches and validating removal of a kickstand, saving approximately $700 per cart.

Tools

Siemens NXTeamcenter PLMFEAElectromechanical integrationControl logicElectronics packagingCabling and harness designConnector definitionDFMPrototype validation

Projects

Work at BrightDrop (General Motors)

Project

Cart tip-over analysis

Validated whether a kickstand was actually required for the Trace Grocery cart by analyzing conservative real-world tipping scenarios before committing to added hardware.

Contribution

  • Proposed tip-over analysis after identifying that the kickstand requirement had been carried over without validation
  • Evaluated worst-case loading with open drawers, downhill grade, child load, and sustained wind
  • Managed external FEA support to confirm the stability analysis and structural assumptions

Result

Eliminated the kickstand from the design, reducing hardware complexity and saving approximately $500 per unit.

Project

Battery and motor controller enclosure

Designed cart-mounted battery and motor controller enclosures through multiple prototype iterations, balancing waterproofing, vibration isolation, ergonomics, service access, and system packaging.

Contribution

  • Designed an injection-molded plastic battery enclosure with shock and vibration isolation for early under-handlebar packaging
  • Integrated an electric latch concept to improve battery replacement speed and support rapid prototype evaluation
  • Repackaged the battery and motor controller under the cart center using a sheet metal enclosure with flip-down service access

Result

Improved component consolidation, user clearance, motor proximity, and battery serviceability while supporting a higher-capacity battery architecture.
